speaker: interviewer
text: All right, let's go into a little bit more detail. Tape two. Let's go into a little bit more detail of your schooling in Asia, in the Russian school and the Polish school.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have much of a schooling there. I mean, we had normal classes. I started from grade one and I went to grade four. We had-- everything was like a normal school, which Polish language was the first language. Second language was-- was Russian. And we learned a little bit of English. I don't know, maybe preparation for my later journeys. But everything was like, we had there-- we got there lunch because we couldn't go back to our homes. You know, a poor lunch for as much as everybody could afford. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And actually it wasn't-- it was a normal school, with Jewish-- Jewish teachers who were professionals. And the school was wonderful. I think it gave me the first good years of schooling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us something, what happened to your father during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, at that time, he was-- as I told you, 1944-- he was there in the labor-- labor camp. We were getting very little communication with him, actually. He wrote maybe once in a while, or they didn't let him write or-- I don't know what was-- what was the reason. He was sick there. He had something with his eyes. And after awhile they had to send him back, otherwise he would probably stay till the-- till the end of the war. And he came back, and he got well, and started again to work. And in 1945-- in, sorry, 1946, we got permission to leave Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, do you remember anything about the end of the war in 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945, I remember-- I don't remember-- I remember only that everybody was happy on the streets and rejoicing and there were bands. This about all what I know. And we were very happy about it. But we still stayed another year in Russia. I don't know what was the reason that we couldn't go back right away. But in 1946 we-- they released us, and we went back to Poland, I remember again in cattle cars. And we passed the boarder, just passing the boarder from Russia to Poland, and the city by the name Przemysl. We stood there for about, I would say, overnight, I think. I don't know the reason why we had to stay there. And we had a pogrom.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do I mean by pogrom? Young people with guns were coming to our cattle, opening with force our cars, and hitting us. Things got-- nothing happened to anybody, but they were just injured a little bit by being hit. But nobody used guns. And they also were robbing and taking things, asking for jewelry, asking-- and we didn't have much. Being what we went through, we didn't have anything, actually. But they were asking. So we gave them whatever anybody had just to get rid of them and not to-- to be hurt. Were these Russians or Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those were Poles. This was right away after we passed the boarder, I don't know how many kilometers after passing the boarder from-- from Russia to Poland. It was 1946. [DRINKS] We finally came to lodge. And then we had some family. My father had distant family that he found, and they took us in. And we started to live a normal life. I went to school. My father had a little store of repairing-- repairing jewelry and watches. And we lived in a lodge. I belonged to an organization. I went to [NON-ENGLISH]. And in 1950-- just a minute, I'm-- 19-- beginning of 1950, we left Poland. The few years in Poland were quite normal, normal life. I was with my parents. We weren't separated. My father made a living. My mother was helping him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any antisemitism there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't-- I cannot say that I did experience anything after the war. Nothing. I went to Polish school. I never was called name-- names. I never, never, never had anything to do with that. But in 19-- it was actually the end of 1949 that we were-- my parents applied for to leave Poland to Israel. And we left-- I don't know why, always-- no, we went in a normal car, normal train. And we went to Italy. And from Italy-- we stayed very short time in Italy. And then we went to Israel. In 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who payed for your passage to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that we paid the passage, that my parents paid the passage to go to Israel. In Israel, when we came to Israel, we went to-- we were also concentrated. All the olim-- all the newcomers were concentrated and sent into different [NON-ENGLISH], if you know what it is. And in this [NON-ENGLISH] we were getting from the government of Israel, we were getting I think beds and some, I don't know exactly what it was, but we got something from to start our lives in those barracks that we were concentrated in. And we stayed there, I stayed in [NON-ENGLISH] which was-- I forgot the name even of the [NON-ENGLISH] at which-- [NON-ENGLISH] right. We were in [NON-ENGLISH] which was not far away from Lod airport. We were there for about six months. It wasn't easy, but we were in our land. And we had to-- to do what everybody else did. We went out from there. My father had some jewelry he sold. I don't know exactly what happened, but we rented an apartment and we lived in Ramat Gan. In Ramat Gan I didn't stay too long because I wanted to go to school. It wasn't easy. My father didn't have a job. My mother didn't have a job. Was very hard in the beginning. But finally he also opened a little store from jewelry and watches. And I went through the government of Israel. It was a service for the oleh. And I went to school to be a nurse. I-- The schools were in also in [NON-ENGLISH], and this was in Be'er Ya'akov. It was a school for girls. We were about 20 girls. And we were working and studying and lived in tents. And we were there over a year. Then we were sent out to all kinds of [NON-ENGLISH] again to work. I was specializing in nursing children-- children diseases in nursing. And I was sent out in [NON-ENGLISH]. That I stayed there for probably a year. And then I came back. After the year, I came back. And I was also working in a hospital in Tel Aviv by the name at that time [NON-ENGLISH] and now it's [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And after that I worked in-- how do you call it? I would say it was a home for unprivileged children. I worked with them also. And I got married to Jack Israeli in 1955. Where did you meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met him in Israel. He is also survivor of Holocaust. He survived. He was born in Eastern Poland. And he survived partly in the forest and partly hidden by farmers. Unfortunately, he is now very sick. He has Alzheimer's disease. I was also divorced from him in 1979.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, well, let's go back to your time in the nursing in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When I was nursing in Israel, it was also a very traumatic thing for all of us because all the olim from-- all the olim from-- from Africa were coming and their children were really, really not taken care of, or they didn't know how to take care of those children. They were dehydrated. They were hungry. They were with sores. Many, many children died because of-- not because of the hospital. Because they were brought to the hospital too late. We tried the best. We worked days and nights to save those children. The doctors were wonderful. The crew of-- of nurses was-- they're very, very dedicated. We were all young people dedicated to our profession and dedicated to-- to our cause, because it was a cause of saving children that sometimes we failed. And it was very painful for us. I don't-- I don't know how to tell you that the life in the beginning of 1950s-- '51, even '52 in Israel wasn't easy for the olim. They lived in [NON-ENGLISH] in-- in tents. It's not because the government didn't provide us with everything. It is also the background of those people who were used to live the way they lived before and it didn't-- they-- they couldn't accept, even, the help, what we were giving to them, because it was so strange and so scary to them. Even to go to a doctor, even to go to a hospital, it was a fear. And that's why so many children unnecessarily were gone. This is what I can tell you about-- about those things, what it was in [NON-ENGLISH] while I was learning, studying there, and working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you said you got married there, so let's go on from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married in 1955. I was still working after I got married and then till I had my first child. And this was in October 19-- October '56 and I had my first child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of my daughter was [? Leora. ?] My husband was working. We had a normal life. We were both working and earning quite nice salaries. And in 19-- my mother, meantime, died in 1956.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel. She was a very young woman. She was only 44 when she died. She died of a heart disease.

speaker: interviewer
text: And by the way, what happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father remained in-- he was working in Israel. And in 19-- end of 1959, we decided to immigrate to Canada. My husband left for United States [DRINKS] a little bit ahead of me. I remained with my father and my daughter. And then I joined him in 19-- beginning of 19-- I made a mistake there. In 1959 I joined him. In 19-- end of 1959, I joined him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you rejoin him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I joined him to-- and I came to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you went to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went through America, yes. Through United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any difficulty getting into Canada from Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. I didn't have any difficulties coming to United States because being a nurse I was on the first preference. So first, actually, I immigrated to-- to United States and then we came from Canada-- to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: What brought you to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was already in Canada and he was starting a little business. He was working here. So he decided that we will stay rather in Canada than the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he doing? What was his work?

speaker: interviewee
text: When he-- in-- in Israel he was working in a-- he was a manager of a factory of hosiery by the name of [NON-ENGLISH]. And then he was a salesmen of all kinds of goods, like dry goods. And when he came to United States, he started to sell bricks. And he was also a salesman for-- insurance salesman. And he started to-- to do something in building business. It was very hard. The beginning was very, very hard for us here. But my husband, my ex-husband, with a lot of work and a lot of vision and a lot of foresight built the empire here. He built a firm by the name of JDS. And he was quite affluent.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was JDS? What kind of firm was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: JDS was Jack and David [? Moscovitch ?]. David [? Moscovitch ?] was his partner. And this was their name of their company. He was very active in the Jewish life here. And in Zionistic life he was a chairman of bonds. He was very active in Ben-Gurion University, in Shoah, in-- actually, in the Lipa building if you-- there is his and his partner, David [? Moscovitch, ?] memorial. The memorial that is in Lipa-- Lipa building, it's theirs. And it was donated by both partners.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the company actually do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The company was sold.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did it perform? What kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Building and development-- developing.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Carry on. What happened at the time of your divorce?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I'll go back because I had another child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her name was Rachel and she was born in 1960. And then in 1967 I had another child, Henry Phillip. Shall I-- all the children are educated, of course. My [? Leora ?] is special education. My Rachel is a lawyer. And Henry is a poet and a screenplay writer. He lives now in New York. My two daughters live in Toronto and have their children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What shall I say more about? I'm now remarried to [NON-ENGLISH] and we live nicely. All right, well, thank you very much. Do you have any photographs or anything that you'd like to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, we'll put those in.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Esther Kafeman. The picture was taken in 1955. Her family perished in Majdanek All the family actually perished in Majdanek. This is my father, Herman Kafeman. The picture was taken in Israel, approximately 1958, '60. Also, my father's family was very large, perished. I don't know exactly where, but partly in Auschwitz and Majdanek. This is my mother, myself, and my uncle before the war, shortly before the war, in a park in Kalisz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been taken in 1938 in the winter time. This is my picture, the only picture that I have before the war. This is probably taken in 19-- beginning of 1939. It's in Kalisz. The rest I don't know. This is my daughter, [? Leora ?] McDonald, with her husband, Ted McDonald, taken in 1980. This is my daughter, Rachel Baron, and Dr. David Baron, her husband, her son, Matthew, and their daughter, Zoe. And this is taken very recently, a week ago. This is my son, Henry Philip Israeli, with his bride, Joanna Goodman. A picture taken very, very recently. This is my granddaughter, Cherise McDonald. And the picture was taken a year ago. On the left is my grandson, Devon McDonald. On the right is [? Noah ?] McDonald, my grandchildren. I want to introduce my husband, [? Edish ?] [NON-ENGLISH] who is a very kind and loving person. And we hope to have our lives together for many, many years. I thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am very happy that I met Bianca 13 years ago. And we are both very happy and we're getting along very well. And I hope many more years we'll be together. Thank you.
